title: GUIDELINES ON THE APPLICATION OF GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF FOOD HYGIENE TO THE CONTROL OF LISTERIA MONOCYTOGENES IN FOODS

sections.section: GUIDELINES ON THE APPLICATION OF GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF FOOD HYGIENE TO THE CONTROL OF LISTERIA MONOCYTOGENES IN FOODS
sections.summary: Document title/heading only: guidelines on applying general food hygiene principles to control Listeria monocytogenes in foods.
Document identifier only: CAC/GL 61-2007 (the codex guideline code).
Table of contents listing document sections and page references; not a substantive regulatory provision.
TOC/heading indicating SECTION I (Objectives) and SECTION II (Scope); no substantive text provided.
Section heading only (Scope); no substantive content in this entry.
Section heading only (Definitions); no substantive definitions provided in this entry.
Section header for PRIMARY PRODUCTION; no substantive requirements in this entry.

For environmental hygiene at primary production this section refers users to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969); no additional measures are specified here.

This section directs readers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for hygienic production of food sources and contains no further specifics.

This section refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for handling, storage and transport and provides no additional requirements.

sections.section: 3.4.CLEANING, MAINTENANCE AND PERSONNEL HYGIENE AT PRIMARY PRODUCTION
sections.summary: This section refers users to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for cleaning, maintenance and personnel hygiene at primary production; no specific measures are listed here.

sections.section: SECTION IV - ESTABLISHMENT: DESIGN AND FACILITIES
sections.summary: Equipment and facilities must be designed, constructed and laid out to ensure cleanability and to minimise Listeria monocytogenes harbourage, cross-contamination and recontamination, with controls such as separation of raw and finished ready‑to‑eat areas (linear flow or physical partitions and appropriate airflow/positive pressure), separate washing areas, condensate/ventilation control, hygienic equipment design to avoid cracks and crevices, isolation and enhanced monitoring during construction/renovation, and routine management of condensate and insulation to prevent harbourage. Where products or ingredients support growth of L. monocytogenes, storage rooms should be designed so product temperature does not exceed 6°C (preferably 2–4°C) and raw materials must be stored separately from finished products.
Section heading for LOCATION; no substantive requirements provided in this entry.
Refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for establishment location guidance; no additional details here.
Equipment should be designed and located to facilitate access for effective cleaning and disinfection to prevent formation of biofilms and L. monocytogenes harbourage sites.
Section heading for PREMISES AND ROOMS; no substantive requirements provided in this entry.

Premises and rooms should, where feasible, be designed to separate raw and finished ready‑to‑eat product areas (e.g., linear product flow, filtered airflow opposite to product flow, physical partitions and higher air pressure on the finished side), provide separate washing areas apart from finished processing and raw ingredient areas, and be maintained as dry as possible because wet operations promote growth and spread of L. monocytogenes.

Because L. monocytogenes can persist in harbourage sites, construction or renovation can reintroduce the organism to the environment, so construction areas should be isolated, hygienic operations reinforced, and environmental monitoring for Listeria spp. increased during works.

This section refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for temporary/mobile premises and vending machines and contains no additional specifics.
Section heading for EQUIPMENT; no substantive text in this entry.

Processing equipment must be designed, constructed and maintained to avoid cracks, crevices, rough welds, hollow supports, inaccessible seals or other harbourage sites for L. monocytogenes; transport racks should have easily cleaned wheel guards, condensate from refrigeration must be directed to drains or drip pans cleaned regularly, and insulation must be installed to avoid becoming harbourage sites.
Refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for food control and monitoring equipment; no further specifics are provided here.

sections.section: 4.3.3 Containers for waste and inedible substances
sections.summary: Non-substantive; directs reader to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for requirements on containers for waste and inedible substances.

Most facility topics (water, drainage, cleaning, personnel facilities, temperature control, lighting, packaging) refer to CAC/RCP 1-1969, while specific provisions require controlling ventilation to minimise condensate/aerosol formation to prevent L. monocytogenes contamination and designing storage rooms (where products support L. monocytogenes growth) so product temperature does not exceed 6°C (preferably 2–4°C) and raw materials are stored separately from finished products.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for water supply requirements.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for drainage and waste disposal requirements.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for cleaning requirements.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for personnel hygiene facilities and toilets.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for temperature control guidance.

Requires control of ventilation to minimise condensate formation because condensate-associated droplets and aerosols have been implicated in L. monocytogenes contamination, and facilities should be designed to prevent such condensate-derived droplets/aerosols from directly or indirectly contaminating food and food-contact surfaces.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for lighting requirements.

Where products or ingredients support L. monocytogenes growth, storage rooms should be designed so product temperature does not exceed 6°C (preferably 2–4°C) and raw materials should be stored separately from finished, processed products.

Processing operations for ready-to-eat products must be controlled to reduce initial contamination and subsequent growth or recontamination by L. monocytogenes throughout production, distribution and consumer handling, using listericidal processes where applicable and complementary measures such as preventing cross-contamination, strict time-temperature control and formulation hurdles where needed.

Control of L. monocytogenes in many ready-to-eat products requires stringent application of Good Hygienic Practice and supportive prerequisite programs together with HACCP, with components of these programs potentially identified as critical control points when L. monocytogenes is a hazard.

sections.section: 5.2 KEY ASPECTS OF HYGIENE CONTROL SYSTEMS
sections.summary: Key hygiene aspects include strict time/temperature control to limit L. monocytogenes growth (with product temperatures not to exceed 6°C preferably 2–4°C and shelf-life based on growth studies) and validation of specific process steps, including listericidal treatments and combinations of intrinsic parameters (e.g., pH, water activity, freezing), plus additional controls or post-packaging listericidal treatments where recontamination before final packaging can occur.

Storage time/temperature must be controlled because refrigerated temperature strongly affects listeriosis risk; product temperature should not exceed 6°C (preferably 2–4°C) and shelf-life for foods that support L. monocytogenes growth must be based on studies that account for possible temperature abuses along the food chain.

Listericidal processes must be validated to ensure consistent effectiveness, and where growth prevention relies on single parameters (e.g., pH <4.4, water activity <0.92, freezing) or combinations of parameters, validation is required; if products that support L. monocytogenes growth can be recontaminated after listericidal treatment, additional measures (e.g., freezing, shorter shelf life, reformulation) or post-packaging listericidal treatments (e.g., heating, high pressure, irradiation where accepted) should be applied as necessary.
Non-substantive; refers to CAC/RCP 1-1969 and CAC/GL 21-1979 for microbiological criteria and specifications for foods.

Microbiological cross-contamination is a major risk for L. monocytogenes and can occur via raw materials, personnel, aerosols, utensils and equipment at any exposed step, so traffic flow should be controlled, equipment and utensils should be dedicated or cleaned/disinfected before entering finished areas, and reusable brines or recycled process water in contact with finished product must be discarded or effectively decontaminated; additionally, consideration should be given to the differing cross-contamination risks of various ready-to-eat foods based on handling and formulation.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for physical and chemical contamination controls.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for incoming material requirements.
Non-substantive; refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for packaging requirements.
Non‑substantive section heading that directs readers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for water-related hygiene guidance.

Refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for requirements and guidance on water in contact with food; no specific measures are provided here.

Refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for use of water as an ingredient; no specific limits or procedures are set in this text.

Directs readers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for hygiene and control measures relating to ice and steam; no substantive requirements are given here.

Refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for management and supervision requirements; this section contains no standalone obligations.

Refers to the Recommended International Code of Practice — General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) for documentation and record‑keeping requirements; no specific record formats or retention periods are specified here.

States that, based on the assessed risk of Listeria monocytogenes in a food product, a decision may be made to recall contaminated product from the market and that public warnings should be considered in some instances.

sections.section: 5.9 MONITORING OF EFFECTIVENESS OF CONTROL MEASURES FOR L. MONOCYTOGENES
sections.summary: Requires an effective environmental monitoring program as an essential component of Listeria control in establishments producing ready‑to‑eat foods, notes that food testing can verify control measures, and refers to Annex 1 for recommendations on environmental monitoring program design.

sections.section: SECTION VI - ESTABLISHMENT: MAINTENANCE AND SANITATION
sections.summary: Sets objectives and rationale for preventive maintenance, targeted cleaning and disinfection, and an environmental monitoring program to reduce contamination and levels of Listeria monocytogenes in ready‑to‑eat foods and to lower the likelihood of post‑processing contamination.
